verses.text: Ben-hadad of Syria besieges Samaria with a great host and demands Ahab's silver, gold, wives, and children. The arrogance of the strong knows no limit. Worldly power, when it has the upper hand, asks for everything and assumes it has the right.

Ben-hadad of Syria besieges Samaria with a great host and demands Ahab's silver, gold, wives, and children. The arrogance of the strong knows no limit. Worldly power, when it has the upper hand, asks for everything and assumes it has the right.

Ben-hadad of Syria besieges Samaria with a great host and demands Ahab's silver, gold, wives, and children. The arrogance of the strong knows no limit. Worldly power, when it has the upper hand, asks for everything and assumes it has the right.

Ahab submits to the first demand, but Ben-hadad raises it — his servants will ransack the city at will. Yielding to a bully only emboldens him to demand more. There comes a point where appeasement merely feeds the appetite it hoped to satisfy.

Ahab submits to the first demand, but Ben-hadad raises it — his servants will ransack the city at will. Yielding to a bully only emboldens him to demand more. There comes a point where appeasement merely feeds the appetite it hoped to satisfy.

Ahab submits to the first demand, but Ben-hadad raises it — his servants will ransack the city at will. Yielding to a bully only emboldens him to demand more. There comes a point where appeasement merely feeds the appetite it hoped to satisfy.

Ahab's elders counsel him not to consent to the second, harsher demand, and he refuses. Even a weak king finds a limit he will not cross. There are insults a people must not accept, and wise counsel helps a ruler know where to stand firm.

Ahab's elders counsel him not to consent to the second, harsher demand, and he refuses. Even a weak king finds a limit he will not cross. There are insults a people must not accept, and wise counsel helps a ruler know where to stand firm.

Ben-hadad answers with a boast, and Ahab replies, 'Let not him that girdeth on his harness boast as he that putteth it off.' Do not crow before the battle is won. The Syrian, drinking and confident, will soon learn that boasting and victory are not the same.

Ben-hadad answers with a boast, and Ahab replies, 'Let not him that girdeth on his harness boast as he that putteth it off.' Do not crow before the battle is won. The Syrian, drinking and confident, will soon learn that boasting and victory are not the same.

Unexpectedly, a prophet comes to wicked Ahab promising victory, 'that thou shalt know that I am the LORD.' God gives the win not for Ahab's sake but for His own glory. The LORD may grant mercy to an undeserving people so that all may know who He is.

While Ben-hadad drinks himself drunk at noon, Ahab's small force goes out, led by young men. The enemy is overconfident and impaired. Pride and self-indulgence leave the mighty unguarded at the very hour of their fall.

While Ben-hadad drinks himself drunk at noon, Ahab's small force goes out, led by young men. The enemy is overconfident and impaired. Pride and self-indulgence leave the mighty unguarded at the very hour of their fall.

While Ben-hadad drinks himself drunk at noon, Ahab's small force goes out, led by young men. The enemy is overconfident and impaired. Pride and self-indulgence leave the mighty unguarded at the very hour of their fall.

Israel strikes, the Syrians flee, and Ahab pursues with great slaughter; Ben-hadad escapes on a horse. The vast host is routed by a handful. When God gives victory, numbers and boasts count for nothing against His purpose.

Israel strikes, the Syrians flee, and Ahab pursues with great slaughter; Ben-hadad escapes on a horse. The vast host is routed by a handful. When God gives victory, numbers and boasts count for nothing against His purpose.

Israel strikes, the Syrians flee, and Ahab pursues with great slaughter; Ben-hadad escapes on a horse. The vast host is routed by a handful. When God gives victory, numbers and boasts count for nothing against His purpose.

The prophet warns Ahab to prepare, for Syria will return; meanwhile Ben-hadad's servants reason their God is a God of the hills, not valleys. Defeated men explain away God instead of fearing Him. Unbelief always finds a theory to dodge the lesson.

The next year Syria attacks in the valley, but God gives Israel another great victory to prove He rules valleys too; even a falling wall kills thousands. The LORD answers the Syrians' blasphemy decisively. He will be known as God everywhere, not in some places only.

Ben-hadad surrenders, and Ahab, flattered, calls him 'my brother,' spares him, and makes a treaty. The king lets go the enemy God had given into his hand. Misplaced mercy toward those God has marked for judgment is not kindness but disobedience.

A prophet, by God's word, has a fellow wound him, then disguises himself with a bandage to set a trap for the king. He acts out a parable to expose Ahab's fault. God's messengers will go to strange lengths to make a blind ruler see his sin.

A prophet, by God's word, has a fellow wound him, then disguises himself with a bandage to set a trap for the king. He acts out a parable to expose Ahab's fault. God's messengers will go to strange lengths to make a blind ruler see his sin.

The prophet's tale traps Ahab into condemning himself: as he let a doomed prisoner go, so his life shall answer for the man God doomed. Ahab goes home 'heavy and displeased.' We are quick to judge a fault in a story and slow to see it is our own.
